pluginAnatomy:
Directory layout (the "general plugin" surface, e.g. calculator example). A plugin lives at one of:
  - Directory plugin: <plugins_root>/<name>/  with  plugin.yaml + __init__.py [+ optional schemas.py, tools.py, skills/..., data files]
  - Entry-point plugin: any pip package that declares a hermes_agent.plugins entry point in pyproject.toml (its top-level package is loaded and register(ctx) is looked up)
  - Specialised tree (different kind: manifest value) lives under plugins/<subsystem>/<name>/ (model-providers/, platforms/, memory/, context_engine/, image_gen/, web/, browser/, observability/, tts/, transcription/, dashboard_auth/, video_gen/, teams_pipeline/, google_meet/, spotify/, kanban/, ...)

Required minimum files for a general plugin:
  plugin.yaml      # manifest (name, version, description; declares provides_tools, provides_hooks, requires_env, kind, author)
  __init__.py      # MUST expose def register(ctx) -> None; if it raises, the plugin is disabled and Hermes continues
  schemas.py       # tool JSON-Schemas (one dict per tool; the 'description' field is what the LLM sees)
  tools.py         # tool handlers, signature def fn(args: dict, **kwargs) -> str, return JSON string, never raise

Optional files (per official guide):
  skills/<name>/SKILL.md   # read-only bundled skills, namespaced <plugin>:<name> via ctx.register_skill()
  any data files           # loaded at import time via Path(__file__).parent
  LICENSE / NOTICE         # legal provenance (e.g. security-guidance forks Anthropic patterns)
  README.md

Discovery roots (per the official guide and Hermes source):
  1. ~/.hermes/plugins/<name>/  (user install)
  2. <hermes-agent>/plugins/<name>/  (bundled, auto-loaded)
  3. Python entry points declared under the group 'hermes_agent.plugins' in any installed package (pyproject.toml)

The PluginManager is the discovery + loader: discover_and_load() -> _discover_and_load_inner() -> _scan_directory() + _scan_entry_points() -> _parse_manifest() -> _load_plugin() imports the module, looks up an attribute named 'register', and calls register(PluginContext(manifest, self)). If register() raises, the plugin is disabled (not fatal to the host).

Bilingual note: code/prompts in English; user-facing descriptions and console/log messages bilingual EN+HU (per project rule).
requiredManifestFields: name (string, required) — the plugin's identifier. Sub-plugins use a dashed 'kind-name' form (e.g. 'web-firecrawl', 'browser-browser-use', 'observability/langfuse').
version (string, required) — typically semver '1.0.0' or quoted '0.1.0'.
description (string, required) — short human-readable summary; used in /plugins list and the banner.
provides_tools (list[string], optional) — tool names this plugin registers. Documented for the calculator example and the plugin guide's 'final plugin structure'.
provides_hooks (list[string], optional) — hook names this plugin subscribes to. Documented in the official guide and used by langfuse/observability.
hooks (list[string], alternative to provides_hooks) — used by security-guidance, observability/langfuse, etc. Treated as equivalent to provides_hooks by the manifest parser.
requires_env (list[string] or list[{name,description,url,secret}], optional) — env-var gate. If unset, plugin is disabled and the user is prompted during 'hermes plugins install'.
kind (string, optional, for backend plugins) — e.g. 'backend' for web-firecrawl/web-brave-free/browser-browser-use. Switches loader to provider-registration path.

provides_web_providers / provides_browser_providers / provides_image_gen_providers / provides_video_gen_providers / provides_tts_providers / provides_transcription_providers / provides_dashboard_auth_providers (list[string], kind-specific) — declares names contributed to those subsystem registries.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_tool(name, toolset, schema, handler, check_fn=None, requires_env=None, is_async=False, description='', emoji='', override=False)
extensionPoints.description: Register a tool in the global tool registry. Signature for handlers: def fn(args: dict, **kwargs) -> str, returning a JSON string and never raising. Pass override=True to replace a built-in tool of the same name. check_fn makes the tool conditional on a runtime check.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: Whenever the plugin exposes a callable capability to the model (custom LLM-callable tool).

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_hook(hook_name, callback)
extensionPoints.description: Subscribe to a lifecycle event. Valid hook names: pre_tool_call, post_tool_call, transform_tool_result, pre_llm_call, post_llm_call, on_session_start, on_session_end, on_session_finalize, on_session_reset, pre_api_request, post_api_request. Only pre_llm_call's return value is consumed (returns {'context': '...'} or a string to inject into the user message; appended, not into system prompt, to preserve prompt cache). transform_tool_result returns an optional string to replace the result. pre_tool_call can return {'action': 'block', 'message': '...'} to refuse.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: Whenever the plugin needs to observe/transform tool calls, LLM calls, or session lifecycle (e.g. security-guidance uses pre_tool_call + transform_tool_result; observability/langfuse uses pre/post_api_request, pre/post_llm_call, pre/post_tool_call).

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_middleware(kind, callback)
extensionPoints.description: Behavior-changing middleware (different from observer hooks). request-middleware rewrites the effective payload; execution-middleware wraps the real callback.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin needs to wrap/transform a request or callback rather than just observe it.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_cli_command(name, help, setup_fn, handler_fn=None, description='')
extensionPoints.description: Register a 'hermes <plugin> ...' CLI subcommand tree. setup_fn receives an argparse subparser.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin needs an out-of-session terminal subcommand (e.g. 'hermes honcho ...').

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_command(name, handler, description='', args_hint='')
extensionPoints.description: Register an in-session slash command (e.g. /mystatus). handler signature: fn(raw_args: str) -> str | None (async supported). Conflicts with built-in commands are rejected. args_hint surfaces argument UI on Discord/etc.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin needs an in-conversation slash command available across CLI and gateway modes.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.dispatch_tool(tool_name, args, **kwargs)
extensionPoints.description: Invoke any tool through the registry with the parent agent's context (CLI: cli.agent attached). Public replacement for hand-rolled agent plumbing.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When a slash command / hook / CLI subcommand needs to call a host tool (e.g. delegate_task) and inherit the parent's context.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_skill(name, path, description='')
extensionPoints.description: Register a read-only skill shipped inside the plugin. The skill is resolvable as '<plugin_name>:<name>' via skill_view(). It does NOT enter the flat global skills index and is not listed in the system prompt's skill index.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin bundles auxiliary SKILL.md workflows it wants the model to be able to load on demand without polluting the global skills listing.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_web_search_provider(provider) / register_browser_provider(provider) / register_image_gen_provider(provider) / register_video_gen_provider(provider) / register_tts_provider(provider) / register_transcription_provider(provider) / register_dashboard_auth_provider(provider)
extensionPoints.description: Register a provider instance for a specialised subsystem. Each provider must inherit from the matching base class (e.g. WebSearchProvider, BrowserProvider, ImageGenProvider, ...). Built-in names win on collision; mis-typed providers are logged at WARNING and ignored.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: For backend-style plugins (kind: backend) that add a new external service to one of the subsystem registries.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_context_engine(engine)
extensionPoints.description: Register a context engine replacing the built-in ContextCompressor. Only one context engine plugin is allowed; the second is rejected.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: For context_engine/<name>/ plugins that want to swap Hermes's default compression strategy.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_platform(name, label, adapter_factory, check_fn, validate_config=None, required_env=None, install_hint='', **entry_kwargs)
extensionPoints.description: Register a gateway/messaging platform adapter (Slack, Discord, Telegram, ...). The adapter_factory must return a BasePlatformAdapter instance.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_slack_action_handler(action_id, callback)
extensionPoints.description: Register a handler for Slack Block Kit click events. async callback must call await ack() within 3 seconds.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin needs to react to Slack button clicks.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.register_auxiliary_task(name, description, defaults=None, plugin=...)
extensionPoints.description: Register an auxiliary task exposed to the agent (used for memory/delegate_task-style routing).
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin exposes a long-running or composable task the host can dispatch.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.llm (property)
extensionPoints.description: Lazy host-owned LLM facade (agent.plugin_llm.PluginLlm) for trusted plugins that need to run chat or structured completions against the user's active model/auth. Override capability (model/agent_id/auth profile) is fail-closed and gated via plugins.entries.<plugin_id>.llm.* config.
extensionPoints.whenToUse: When the plugin needs to call the user's LLM without bringing its own provider keys.

extensionPoints.name: ctx.inject_message(content, role='user')
extensionPoints.description: Inject a message into the active conversation. Interrupts a running turn or queues as next input if idle. Not available in gateway mode (returns False).
extensionPoints.whenToUse: For remote-control viewers / messaging bridges that push messages into the conversation.

examples.name: plugins/security-guidance (general plugin, hook-only)
examples.whatItDoes: Single-directory plugin with plugin.yaml + __init__.py + patterns.py + LICENSE/NOTICE + README.md. register(ctx) calls ctx.register_hook('pre_tool_call', ...) and ctx.register_hook('transform_tool_result', ...). Manifest declares hooks: [transform_tool_result, pre_tool_call]. Demonstrates the warn-vs-block toggle via SECURITY_GUIDANCE_BLOCK env var.

examples.name: plugins/web/firecrawl (kind: backend, sub-plugin)
examples.whatItDoes: Sub-plugin under plugins/web/. plugin.yaml: name: web-firecrawl, kind: backend, provides_web_providers: [firecrawl]. __init__.py: register(ctx) -> ctx.register_web_search_provider(FirecrawlWebSearchProvider()). provider.py holds the actual class. Auto-loaded by kind.

examples.name: plugins/web/brave_free (kind: backend, sub-plugin)
examples.whatItDoes: Mirrors the image_gen/<vendor>/ layout: provider.py holds the class, __init__.py:register(ctx) instantiates and registers it. plugin.yaml: name: web-brave-free, kind: backend, provides_web_providers: [brave-free].

examples.name: plugins/browser/browser_use (kind: backend, sub-plugin)
examples.whatItDoes: Backend browser provider. plugin.yaml: name: browser-browser-use, kind: backend, provides_browser_providers: [browser-use]. __init__.py: register(ctx) -> ctx.register_browser_provider(BrowserUseBrowserProvider()).

examples.name: plugins/observability/langfuse (general plugin, many hooks)
examples.whatItDoes: Opt-in via 'hermes plugins enable observability/langfuse'. plugin.yaml: name: langfuse, requires_env: [HERMES_LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY, HERMES_LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY], hooks: [pre_api_request, post_api_request, pre_llm_call, post_llm_call, pre_tool_call, post_tool_call]. Demonstrates the env-var gate + many hook subscriptions in one plugin.

examples.name: Official calculator example (the canonical 'general plugin' template)
examples.whatItDoes: Two tools (calculate, unit_convert), a post_tool_call hook, and a bundled skill. Files: plugin.yaml + __init__.py + schemas.py + tools.py + skills/.... register(ctx) calls ctx.register_tool twice + ctx.register_hook once. Demonstrates the most common skeleton a new plugin should follow.

patchHookMechanism:
There is NO clean plugin hook for monkey-patching a hardcoded constant in Hermes core. Verified by reading the full PluginContext class (hermes_cli/plugins.py, lines 290-1090) and the official 'build-a-hermes-plugin.md' guide:

  - PluginContext only exposes register_* methods (register_tool, register_hook, register_middleware, register_command, register_cli_command, register_skill, register_web_search_provider, register_browser_provider, register_image_gen_provider, register_video_gen_provider, register_tts_provider, register_transcription_provider, register_dashboard_auth_provider, register_context_engine, register_platform, register_slack_action_handler, register_auxiliary_task) plus llm and inject_message.
  - The 60-character rule is a DOCUMENTATION hardline in AGENTS.md § 'Skill authoring standards' (HARDLINE) and CONTRIBUTING.md § 'Skill authoring standards' (HARDLINE). The constant that backs the runtime truncation is tools.skills_tool.MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH = 1024 (lines 95, 655-656, 810-811) and a parallel tools.skill_manager_tool.MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH = 1024 (lines 112, 246-247). These are used in skills_tool.py and skill_manager_tool.py.
  - There is no hook named 'pre_skills_load' or 'patch_constant' or any 'transform_skill_index' surface. There is no PluginContext method to mutate tools.* module attributes.
  - The only ways a plugin *could* in principle reach MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH:
      (a) Module-level monkey-patch: in the plugin's __init__.py, do 'import tools.skills_tool as _s; _s.MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH = 1024' — but the tools.skills_tool module is imported lazily on first use of the skill listing; if the plugin registers first, its mutation would apply. This is unsupported, fragile (depends on import order), and explicitly out of band — there is no documented hook for it.
      (b) A transform_tool_result / pre_tool_call hook does not apply — the description truncation happens during SKILL.md parse in the skill listing loader, not in a tool result.
      (c) A pre_llm_call hook returning context that nudges the model toward shorter descriptions — purely advisory, does not actually change the truncation constant.

Recommended cleanest approach (ranked):

  1. RECOMMENDED — Do not patch the constant at all. Treat the 60-character limit as a STYLE guideline at skill-writing time and rely on the existing 1024-char safety truncation in tools/skills_tool.py:655-656. The runtime code already accepts up to 1024 chars in frontmatter; the 60-char rule is enforced by code review, not by the runtime. Verify with the 'assert len(m.group(1)) <= 60' snippet already shipped in AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md.
  2. If raising the runtime cap is genuinely required, the supported mechanism is to ship a fork of Hermes that edits tools/skills_tool.py:95 (and the matching MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH in tools/skill_manager_tool.py:112). The patch script referenced by the project (Phase 5 / Task B) is exactly that — a controlled, idempotent edit of the constant in the user's ~/.hermes/hermes-agent checkout, with a backup and a profile. There is no plugin-level hook for this.
  3. If the project's 'plugin' must do something at runtime, an OPT-IN plugin can register a pre_llm_call hook that injects a brief reminder into the user message telling the model to keep descriptions under 60 chars. This does NOT change the truncation constant — it changes model behaviour. Acceptable as a soft companion to (1).
  4. A monkey-patch from __init__.py (option (a) above) is technically possible but undocumented, depends on import order, and is not a supported hook. Treat as a last resort and document the fragility.

In short: no clean plugin hook exists for raising the 60/1024 char limit. The 60-char rule is a documentation/UX hardline (AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md 'HARDLINE' sections); the 1024-char value is a Python constant in two files (tools/skills_tool.py:95 and tools/skill_manager_tool.py:112). The supported way to change it is a controlled source edit (patch script) of those two constants; the supported plugin surface is only for register_* methods, none of which can mutate a tools.* module constant.
evidence:
Official documentation (read-only fetch):
  - website/docs/guides/build-a-hermes-plugin.md — full plugin guide (calculator example, manifest fields, hook reference, specialised plugin types, non-Python extension surfaces, distribution via entry point / NixOS, common mistakes). Captured in this turn.
  - website/docs/guides/ — sibling guides (work-with-skills.md, python-library.md, ...) available; not needed for plugin authoring spec.
  - website/docs/reference/ — reference docs available; not needed for plugin authoring spec.

Hermes source (read-only, paths under /Users/kiscsicska/.hermes/hermes-agent/):
  - plugins/__init__.py: bare package marker (1 line: '# Hermes plugins package'). Confirms plugins is a namespace package loaded by name.
  - plugins/plugin_utils.py: ships lazy_singleton decorator + SingletonSlot class. Thread-safe lazy caching helpers for plugin authors. No state, no side effects, import-light.
  - hermes_cli/plugins.py: contains PluginContext class (lines 290-1090) with the full register_* surface enumerated above. Validates unknown hook/middleware names with a warning rather than raising (forward-compat). PluginManager.discover_and_load -> _discover_and_load_inner -> _scan_directory + _scan_entry_points -> _parse_manifest -> _load_plugin (imports module, looks up 'register' attribute, calls register(PluginContext)). If register() raises, the plugin is disabled but the host continues.
  - plugins/security-guidance/{plugin.yaml, __init__.py, patterns.py, LICENSE, NOTICE, README.md}: the canonical 'single-directory hook-only plugin' example. Manifest: name, version, description, author, hooks: [transform_tool_result, pre_tool_call]. register() calls ctx.register_hook twice. Uses SECURITY_GUIDANCE_BLOCK env var to switch warn vs block.
  - plugins/web/__init__.py: docstring describes the sub-plugin convention: plugins/web/<name>/{plugin.yaml, __init__.py, provider.py}, auto-loaded by kind: backend via ctx.register_web_search_provider().
  - plugins/web/firecrawl/{plugin.yaml, __init__.py, provider.py}: sub-plugin. kind: backend, provides_web_providers: [firecrawl].
  - plugins/web/brave_free/{plugin.yaml, __init__.py, provider.py}: sub-plugin. kind: backend, provides_web_providers: [brave-free].
  - plugins/browser/browser_use/{plugin.yaml, __init__.py, provider.py}: sub-plugin. kind: backend, provides_browser_providers: [browser-use].
  - plugins/observability/langfuse/plugin.yaml: opt-in plugin with requires_env gate and 6 hook subscriptions. Demonstrates env-gated, multi-hook plugin pattern.
  - tools/skills_tool.py:95, 655-656, 810-811: MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH = 1024 (the runtime constant that backs skill-description truncation).
  - tools/skill_manager_tool.py:112, 246-247: parallel MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH = 1024 (hard-fails writes exceeding it).
  - AGENTS.md:859, CONTRIBUTING.md:520: 'description ≤ 60 characters, one sentence, ends with a period' marked HARDLINE; 'Reviewers reject PRs that violate them.' Includes the 'assert len(m.group(1)) <= 60' verification snippet.

Verified non-existence of patch hooks:
  - PluginContext has no method to mutate tools/skills_tool.MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH.
  - The official 'Hook reference' table in build-a-hermes-plugin.md lists 8 hooks: pre_tool_call, post_tool_call, pre_llm_call, post_llm_call, on_session_start, on_session_end, on_session_finalize, on_session_reset. None applies to skill-index loading.
  - The 'Non-Python extension surfaces' section lists MCP servers, gateway event hooks (HOOK.yaml + handler.py in ~/.hermes/hooks/<name>/), shell hooks in config.yaml, skill taps, and TTS/STT command templates. None of these mutate a Python constant in tools/*.
  - Conclusion: there is no clean plugin-level monkey-patch surface for raising the description limit. The 60-char rule is a documentation hardline; the 1024-char constant is editable only via a controlled source edit (the patch script that Phase 5 / Task B will implement).
